query: histologic findings in vanishing testes syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histological examination of a testis biopsy is the definitive method of diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Favorable indicators are younger age, normal-sized testes, no fertility issues in the female partner, focal disease, and the presence of any visible sperm on histological examination of the testis biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The specific diagnosis for non-obstructive azoospermia is typically made by histological appearance and generally requires a testis biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Ambiguous Genitalia and Disorders of Sexual Differentiation -- Evaluation -- Individuals with 46 XY and Disorders of Sexual Development
retrieved_docs.content: In other individuals, the presence of testicular tissue can be ascertained by either performing ultrasound or measuring the concentration of anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH), which serves as a marker of testicular tissue. In patients with low AMH, gonadal dysgenesis, testicular regression syndrome, vanishing testicular syndrome, or persistent Mullerian duct syndrome are likely causes.

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Surgeons should try to find a dilated and opaque seminiferous tubule.

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Four different types of Sertoli cells have been described. [22] Sertoli cell-specific maturation markers can be used for cellular differentiation, but light and electron microscopy are used more frequently. [22] [23] [24] Histological differentiation of Sertoli cells can generally be done according to these criteria: Normal mature cells which typically demonstrate a vaguely triangular shape and a prominent nucleolus Immature cells with round nuclei but immature cytoplasm Dysgenetic cells with immature nuclei Involuted cells with irregular nuclei [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Potter Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histopathology study of lungs shows hypoplasia with inadequate parenchymal development, and in many cases, the respiratory tract and alveoli are lined by cuboidal epithelium. But some cases have no histopathological abnormalities. [1] [3] Hyaline membrane formation is prominent in respiratory distress syndrome. [8] Histopathology of gonads reveals hypoplastic gonads with undeveloped seminiferous tubules. [3] Microscopy of renal tissue may show primitive glomeruli, cysts formation with cuboidal or squamous epithelium lining with fluids within dilated cysts. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Micropenis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vanishing testes syndrome. [2] [8] [31] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Evaluation -- Testicular Etiologies
retrieved_docs.content: Other causes of azoospermia include XYY syndrome, myotonic dystrophy, Noonan syndrome, 5 alpha-reductase deficiency, androgen insensitivity syndrome, and vanishing testis syndrome. There is sufficient evidence to confirm that varicoceles have a deleterious impact on the semen and testes, while varicocelectomy has been shown to improve sperm parameters and testicular function. [65] [66] [67] However, only a small percentage of men with azoospermia due to testicular failure will benefit from surgical treatment of their clinical varicocele. [68]

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Primary or pure Sertoli cell-only syndrome is generally thought to arise from a disorder of gonocyte migration that fails to reach the gonads. This causes uniform or primary Sertoli cell-only syndrome with no focal spermatogenesis. [1] [6] [20] [22] Seminiferous tubular lumen size is smaller than normal, but the walls are not thickened. Only small amounts of Sertoli cell cytoplasmic lipids are seen. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: It is vital to properly rule out other conditions that may present with a low sperm count. Some of the differentials to consider are: Azoospermia from other causes Cystic fibrosis Leydig cell hyperplasia Klinefelter syndrome Maturational arrest Post bilateral vasectomy End-stage testis failure Sexual dysfunction Severe oligospermia Hypospermatogenesis Varicocele Improper specimen collection or handling

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The patients are typically young males aged 20 to 40 who present for an infertility evaluation. The testes can be normal or slightly smaller in size and have a normal consistency and shape, although some patients have been reported to show some testicular atrophy. There may be a history of male infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: Semen Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Low Sperm Count
retrieved_docs.content: Low sperm count, high testosterone, high LH, and normal FSH: This pattern could indicate partial androgen insensitivity  syndrome in men. Men with this condition may have gynecomastia and display a range of genital presentations, from hypospadias to normal male genitalia, due to improper androgen receptor function. [17] Low sperm count, normal testosterone, normal LH, normal FSH, and normal testis size: Evaluate for genital tract obstruction: Congenital absence of the vas deferens, identified through a physical examination and low semen pH, and confirmed on scrotal or transrectal ultrasound, is often associated with cystic fibrosis mutations confirmed by genetic testing or an absent kidney. [18] [19] Ejaculatory duct obstruction can also be secondary to infections such as chlamydia, gonorrhea, tuberculosis, or vasectomy. In such cases, scrotal or transrectal ultrasound reveals dilated seminal vesicles. [20] Sperm, in such cases, has poor progressive motility. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Neither hormonal concentrations, histological diagnosis, testicular volume, age, testosterone, LH, nor FSH levels are sufficiently reliable enough to predict the presence of sperm on micro-TESE although there is a slight overall suggestion that higher FSH levels might indicate a somewhat better successful retrieval rate. [1] [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In Sertoli cell-only syndrome, all seminiferous tubules are lined exclusively with Sertoli cells. No germ cells are present, although there may be focal areas of normal, preserved spermatogenesis somewhere in the testis. [6] Compared to obstructive azoospermia, patients with Sertoli cell-only syndrome have thicker basement membranes with narrower lumens. [19] Extracellular matrix (laminin) is also increased, possibly due to inhibited spermatogenesis. [19] There is also a significant decrease of seminal laminin in Sertoli cell-only syndrome, which has been suggested as a possible screening test. [19] There are two histological types of Sertoli cell-only syndrome; primary and secondary. [1] [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Testicle -- Pathophysiology -- Anorchia
retrieved_docs.content: By definition, anorchia is the absence of testes in a person who has a 46 XY karyotype and an external male phenotype. It is also known as vanishing testis syndrome or embryonic testicular regression. [98] [99] [100] The exact etiology of congenital anorchia is not well known. However, there appears to be a genetic component due to the familial inheritance associated with anorchia. [98] However, in previous studies, mutations in genes such as SRY, INSL3/INSL3 receptor do not have an association with anorchia. [101] But mutations in genes such as NR5A1, which codes for SF1, correlate with anorchia. [102]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder of the male reproductive system encompasses a range of conditions affecting male reproductive organs, and within this category testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية) represents a specific disease entity. Cancer of the testis is synonymous with testicular cancer and therefore also belongs to the broader group of male reproductive system disorders. This hierarchical relationship clarifies that both the general classification “Disorder of male reproductive system” and the specific term “cancer of testis” are linked under the specific condition identified as سرطـان الخصية.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1882
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE GENITOURINARY SYSTEM is positioned within the broader category of cancer of testis, indicating that this specific classification of genitourinary neoplasms is a subset of testicular malignancy. Cancer of testis, in turn, is classified under the overarching term سرطان الخصية (testicular cancer), establishing سرطان الخصية as the parent entity for this disease. Additionally, the Disorder of male reproductive system is also linked to سرطان الخصية as a parent category, reflecting that disorders affecting the male reproductive tract are encompassed within the larger framework of testicular cancer. Together, these hierarchical relationships clarify that both the detailed clinical classification of genitourinary neoplasms and general male reproductive system disorders are integrated under the umbrella of سرطان الخصية, emphasizing the central role of testicular cancer in organizing related diagnostic and pathological concepts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1539
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is morphologically associated with obstruction (انسداد), indicating that structural abnormalities characteristic of this syndrome often involve obstructive processes. Chronic asthmatic bronchitis is also associated with obstruction, reflecting the contribution of airway narrowing and mucus plugging to the disease’s clinical picture. The concept of obstruction (انسداد) itself is linked to several specific obstructive conditions: urinary tract obstruction (انسداد السبيل البولي), a yellowish stagnation state (رُّكودِ صفْراوِيّ), the entity عِلَّوس, renal obstruction (موه الكلى), and bladder obstruction (انسداد المثانة). Together, these relationships highlight that both genetic syndromes such as Bard‑Pic and chronic respiratory diseases like asthmatic bronchitis can involve or predispose to a range of localized obstructive pathologies affecting the urinary system, kidneys, bladder, and other tissues, underscoring the importance of recognizing and managing obstruction in diverse clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 593
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE GENITOURINARY SYSTEM is organized under two major categories: cancer of other male genital organs and cancer of the testis. Within the hierarchy of male genital neoplasms, cancer of other male genital organs serves as the parent category for penile cancer (سرطان قضيبي), while cancer of the testis is the parent category for testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية). Additionally, the broader entity Disorder of male reproductive system is classified as a subcategory of testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية). This hierarchical arrangement links the clinical classification of genitourinary neoplasms to specific organ‑specific cancers, clarifying how penile and testicular malignancies fit within the overall taxonomy of male reproductive system disorders and supporting more precise diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2319
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method includes the value “الخزعة” (biopsy). Diagnostic endocrine procedures have a broader relationship with الخزعة, indicating that biopsy can be employed within endocrine diagnostic contexts. The category diagnostic procedures; male genital lists الخزعة as a method, showing that biopsy is a technique used to evaluate male genital conditions. Transrectal biopsy of prostate is connected to عمليات البروستاتا (prostate procedures) through a broader‑relationship link, and it is also a child entity of diagnostic procedures; male genital, establishing it as a specialized male genital diagnostic procedure. Together, these relationships illustrate that الخزعة is a fundamental biospecimen collection method across multiple diagnostic domains—including endocrine and male genital assessments—and that the Transrectal biopsy of prostate exemplifies its specific application within prostate‑related procedures.
